data.title: Cardiff Oncology appoints Mani Mohindru as CEO
data.description:
• Cardiff Oncology (CRDF) on Thursday said it has appointed Mani Mohindru as President and Chief Executive Officer.
  • Mohindru, who was serving as the interim CEO of the company, will continue as a member of the Board. 
  • The company also appointed Josh Muntner as Chief Financial Officer and Ajay Aggarwal as Chief Operating Officer, effective April 6 and April 27, respectively.
  • Previously, Muntner served as CFO of Imvax and Mesoblast, while most recently, Aggarwal served as Senior Vice President and Head of Clinical Development at Aclaris Therapeutics, where he led clinical strategy and execution across multiple programs.
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: Abu Dhabi Oil CEO says Strait of Hormuz not open, under Iran control
data.description:
Abu Dhabi National Oil CEO Sultan Ahmed Al Jaber said the Strait of Hormuz is not truly open, with Iran still controlling access.

"This moment requires clarity. So let’s be clear: the Strait of Hormuz is not open. Access is being restricted, conditioned, and controlled," Jaber said in a long post on LinkedIn Thursday.

"Iran has made clear—through both its statements and actions—that passage is subject to permission, conditions, and political leverage. That is not freedom of navigation. That is coercion," Jaber said. "Conditional passage is not passage. It is control by another name."

Jaber noted that around 230 oil transport vessels are loaded and waiting to exit via the strait, adding that "no country has a legitimate right to determine who may pass and under what terms."

Abu Dhabi National Oil Company, or ADNOC, is the state-owned oil company of the United Arab Emirates.

data.title: Nouveau Monde Graphite secures $297 million equity financing to advance Matawinie project
data.description:
• Nouveau Monde Graphite (NMG) said on Thursday it has arranged a $297 million equity financing package to support development of its Phase-2 Matawinie Mine.
  • The financing includes a $213 million private placement backed by Canada Growth Fund, Investissement Québec and Eni, alongside an $84 million bought deal public offering of subscription receipts.
  • Combined with a previously announced $335 million debt commitment, the company said the funding is expected to fully finance the project and support a final investment decision.
  • The private placement is expected to close around May 15, subject to shareholder and regulatory approvals.
  • NMG +8.93% after hours to $2.56.
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: Cars.com accelerates AI push, cuts costs, boosts buyback plan
data.description:
• Cars.com (CARS) outlined 2026 initiatives focused on AI innovation, cost efficiency, and organizational restructuring.
  • Launched new AI-powered tools including the Dealer App and Advanced Shopper Alerts to enhance dealer sales and analytics.
  • Initiated cost-cutting measures including an ~11% workforce reduction, targeting $25–30M in annual savings by 2027.
  • Flattened management structure, with ~20% of eliminated roles from leadership layers.
  • Increased 2026 share repurchase target from $60M+ to $90M, with ~$24M already completed.
  • Reaffirmed 2026 guidance, expecting flat to modest revenue growth and stable EBITDA margins.

data.title: Further inflation spikes are likely as Middle East conflict does not reach resolution
data.description:
Market volatility continues as investors grapple with geopolitical uncertainty and stubborn inflation data, according to Leo Nelissen, Investing Group Leader for Main Street Alpha.

“Today was, once again, about the binary nature of the market, as investors know the rally fully depends on a lasting ceasefire in Iran,” Nelissen said.

He noted that with Iran’s demands unlikely to be met by the U.S. and its partners, combined with escalating tensions in Lebanon, “investors know the path forward is bumpy, which makes further inflation spikes likely.”

Recent economic data reinforces these concerns. The February Core PCE Price Index—the Federal Reserve’s preferred inflation measure—rose 0.4% month-over-month, coming in hotter than the 0.3% consensus. The annual reading of 3.0% marks the 60th consecutive month above the Fed’s 2% target.

Meanwhile, U.S. Q4 GDP growth was revised down to just 0.5%, and initial jobless claims exceeded expectations.

Charlie Bilello, chief market strategist at Creative Planning, argued that current conditions make “a strong case for a rate hike as inflation rates are about to spike higher.”

Peter Boockvar, chief investment officer at OnePoint BFG Wealth Partners, predicts inflation will remain elevated at 3-4% through year-end, suggesting any rate cut would be “more symbolic than anything.”

IMF Managing Director Kristalina Georgieva offered a stark warning: “All roads go into higher inflation, slower growth.”

Lastly, former investment banker Marc-André Fongern emphasized that until the Strait of Hormuz returns to normal, “the war isn’t over; and, in theory, it shouldn’t be over for market pricing either.”

More on the U.S. economy, inflation

  • Core PCE inflation comes in slightly hotter than expected in February
  • Fed officials see higher risk in inflation and labor market, while the Iran war clouds outlook: FOMC minutes
  • New York Fed's Williams sees core inflation remaining at ~2.5% this year, despite oil price surge

data.title: Apollo's Rowan resolves JPMorgan suit regarding Charlie Javice fraud - report
data.description:
Apollo Global Management (APO) CEO Marc Rowan resolved a lawsuit that JPMorgan Chase (JPM) had filed against him and other early investors in Charlie Javice's student-finance startup, Frank, according to a media report on Thursday.

Javice was convicted in March 2025 of defrauding the bank, which acquired the startup for $175M. In the lawsuit against Rowan and others, JPMorgan (JPM) was seeking to recoup some of its losses from the backers. A trust controlled by Rowan, who personally invested in Frank, was one of the defendants named in a sealed complaint JPM filed last month.

On Wednesday, JPMorgan (JPM) said in a court filing that it is voluntarily dismissing claims against the Rowan trust, Bloomberg News reported on Thursday. The dismissal came the same day that a redacted version of the bank's lawsuit became available to the public.

Israeli venture capital fund Aleph LP was also sued by JPMorgan. Its founder, Michael Eisenberg, had introduced Javice to the bank, according to a JPMorgan executive's testimony at Javice's trial.

In JPMorgan's suit, language describing an agreement that JPM says establishes backers' liability for losses resulting from fraud was redacted, Bloomberg said. The filing said the bank asked investors to honor the agreement soon after it discovered Javice's fraud in 2022. They initially refused and did not change their stance even after she was convicted.

Javice was sentenced to seven years in prison but is free on bail while appealing her conviction, Bloomberg said.

JPMorgan (JPM) believed the college financial planning website Frank had ~4.3M customers when it paid $175M for the business. Instead, it had fewer than 300K registered users.

data.title: U.S. Postal Service seeks stamp price increase to 82 cents as costs rise
data.description:
The U.S. Postal Service on Thursday proposed a new round of mailing price increases, including a hike in the cost of a First-Class Mail Forever stamp from 78 cents to 82 cents, according to a filing submitted to the Postal Regulatory Commission.

The changes, if approved, would take effect July 12 and raise mailing services prices by roughly 4.8 percent overall.

First-class mail prices move higher

Under the proposal, the price of sending a one-ounce letter would rise by four cents, while metered letters would increase from 74 cents to 78 cents. Domestic postcard rates would climb from 61 cents to 65 cents, and international postcards and one-ounce international letters would both increase from $1.70 to $1.75.

The additional-ounce charge for single-piece letters would remain unchanged at 29 cents.

The Postal Service is also seeking adjustments across a broader set of offerings, including periodicals, marketing mail, package services and certain special services products.

Filing reflects ongoing financial pressure

The rate request comes as the Postal Service continues to face persistent financial challenges and higher operating costs. The agency said it is relying on its regulatory authority to adjust prices as part of efforts to maintain operations and meet its universal service mandate.

Unlike many federal entities, the Postal Service does not receive regular taxpayer funding for operating expenses and instead depends primarily on revenue from postage and related services.

Even with the proposed increases, the agency said U.S. mailing prices would remain comparatively low by international standards, subject to review by the commission.

data.title: Cohen & Steers sees a decrease in AUM as of March end
data.description:
Cohen & Steers (CNS) on Thursday said its preliminary AUM as of March 31 stood at $93.1B, down from $98.4B at the end of February.

The decrease was due to market depreciation of $5B and distributions of $323M, partially offset by net inflows of $42M.

By investment vehicle, Institutional Accounts ended the month with $38.5B in AUM, Open-end Funds with $47.1B, and Closed-end Funds with $12.8B.

data.title: TriNet to acquire Cocoon to expand leave management offerings
data.description:
• TriNet (TNET) (NYSE: TNET) on Thursday said it has agreed to acquire leave management technology provider Cocoon, in a deal aimed at strengthening its services for small and medium-sized businesses.
  • Cocoon will add a compliance-focused leave of absence platform with automated workflows and AI-enabled capabilities to TriNet’s offerings.

  • The transaction is expected to close this month, subject to customary conditions and regulatory approvals. 

  • Financial terms were not disclosed, and TriNet said the deal is not expected to materially impact its 2026 financial guidance.
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: Texas Pacific Land plunges after CEO of top shareholder Horizon Kinetics dies
data.description:
Texas Pacific Land (TPL) closed down 15.6% to a two-month low on Thursday following news that Murray Stahl, CEO of Horizon Kinetics Holding (HKHC) and a Texas Pacific board member, died suddenly on April 7.

Horizon owns ~15% of Texas Pacific’s (TPL) outstanding shares, making it the energy real estate operating company's largest shareholder, according to data compiled by Bloomberg.

"Horizon Kinetics, along with its predecessors, had been TPL's largest shareholder for many decades. Murray believed in the company when it was still a thinly traded, little-known trust that simply owned some land in west Texas," Texas Pacific (TPL) CEO Ty Glover said.

LandBridge (LB), another Horizon holding, slumped 7% on Thursday, but shares of WaterBridge Infrastructure (WBI), another large Horizon holding according to data compiled by Bloomberg, fell only 0.2%; Horizon Kinetics (HKHC) shares plunged 12.7%.
